solution:
Solution 1. The polynomial \( x^{3}-3 x+1 \) changes sign in each of the intervals \( [-2,-1],[1 / 3,1 / 2],[3 / 2,2] \), so it has no zeros outside these intervals. Hence in the problem, the integrand is continuous on the three ranges of integration.

By the substitutions \( x=1 /(1-t) \) and \( x=1-1 / t \), the integrals over \( [1 / 101,1 / 11] \) and \( [101 / 100,11 / 10] \) are respectively converted into integrals over \( [-100,-10] \). The integrand
\[
Q(x)=\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right)^{2}
\]
is invariant under each of the substitutions \( x \rightarrow 1 /(1-x) \) and \( x \rightarrow 1-1 / x \). Hence the sum of the three given integrals is expressible as
\[
\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-x)^{2}}\right) d x .
\]

But
\[
\frac{1}{Q(x)}=\left(x+1-\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x-1}\right)^{2},
\]
so the last integral is of the form \( \int u^{-2} d u \). Hence its value is
\[
-\left.\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10},
\]
which is rational.
Solution 2. Set
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(t)= & \int_{-100}^{t}\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right)^{2} d x+\int_{\frac{1}{101}}^{1 /(1-t)}\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right)^{2} d x \\
& +\int_{\frac{101}{100}}^{1-1 / t}\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right)^{2} d x
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( -100 \leq t \leq-10 \). We want \( f(-10) \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
\[
f^{\prime}(t)=Q(t)+Q\left(\frac{1}{1-t}\right) \frac{1}{(1-t)^{2}}+Q\left(1-\frac{1}{t}\right) \frac{1}{t^{2}} .
\]

We find that \( Q(1 /(1-x))=Q(1-1 / x)=Q(x)( \) in fact \( x \mapsto 1 /(1-x) \) and \( x \mapsto 1-1 / x \) are inverses of each other), so
\[
f(-10)=\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-x)^{2}}\right) d x .
\]

The integrand equals
\[
\frac{x^{4}-2 x^{3}+3 x^{2}-2 x+1}{\left(x^{3}-3 x+1\right)^{2}} .
\]

We guess that this is the derivative of a quotient
\[
\frac{A x^{2}+B x+C}{x^{3}-3 x+1}
\]
and solve for the undetermined coefficients, obtaining \( A=-1, B=1, C=0 \). Thus
\[
f(-10)=\left.\frac{-x^{2}+x}{x^{3}-3 x+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10}
\]
which is rational.

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. The polynomial \( unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1 \) changes sign in each of the intervals \( [-2,-1],[1 / 3,1 / 2],[3 / 2,2] \), so it has no zeros outside these intervals. Hence in the problem, the integrand is continuous on the three ranges of integration.

By the substitutions \( unknownx=1 /(1-unknownt) \) and \( unknownx=1-1 / unknownt \), the integrals over \( [1 / 101,1 / 11] \) and \( [101 / 100,11 / 10] \) are respectively converted into integrals over \( [-100,-10] \). The integrand
\[
integrandfunc(unknownx)=\left(\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right)^{2}
\]
is invariant under each of the substitutions \( unknownx \rightarrow 1 /(1-unknownx) \) and \( unknownx \rightarrow 1-1 / unknownx \). Hence the sum of the three given integrals is expressible as
\[
\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{unknownx^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-unknownx)^{2}}\right) d unknownx .
\]

But
\[
\frac{1}{integrandfunc(unknownx)}=\left(unknownx+1-\frac{1}{unknownx}-\frac{1}{unknownx-1}\right)^{2},
\]
so the last integral is of the form \( \int unknownu^{-2} d unknownu \). Hence its value is
\[
-\left.\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10},
\]
which is rational.

Solution 2. Set
\[
\begin{aligned}
sumfunction(unknownt)= & \int_{-100}^{\unknownt}\left(\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right)^{2} d unknownx+\int_{\frac{1}{101}}^{1 /(1-\unknownt)}\left(\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right)^{2} d unknownx \\
& +\int_{\frac{101}{100}}^{1-1 / \unknownt}\left(\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right)^{2} d unknownx
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( -100 \leq \unknownt \leq-10 \). We want \( sumfunction(-10) \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
\[
sumfunction^{\prime}(\unknownt)=integrandfunc(\unknownt)+integrandfunc\left(\frac{1}{1-\unknownt}\right) \frac{1}{(1-\unknownt)^{2}}+integrandfunc\left(1-\frac{1}{\unknownt}\right) \frac{1}{\unknownt^{2}} .
\]

We find that \( integrandfunc(1 /(1-unknownx))=integrandfunc(1-1 / unknownx)=integrandfunc(unknownx)( \) in fact \( unknownx \mapsto 1 /(1-unknownx) \) and \( unknownx \mapsto 1-1 / unknownx \) are inverses of each other), so
\[
sumfunction(-10)=\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{unknownx^{2}-unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{unknownx^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-unknownx)^{2}}\right) d unknownx .
\]

The integrand equals
\[
\frac{unknownx^{4}-2 unknownx^{3}+3 unknownx^{2}-2 unknownx+1}{\left(unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1\right)^{2}} .
\]

We guess that this is the derivative of a quotient
\[
\frac{coeffalpha \, unknownx^{2}+coeffbeta \, unknownx+coeffgamma}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}
\]
and solve for the undetermined coefficients, obtaining \( coeffalpha=-1, coeffbeta=1, coeffgamma=0 \). Thus
\[
sumfunction(-10)=\left.\frac{-unknownx^{2}+unknownx}{unknownx^{3}-3 unknownx+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10}
\]
which is rational.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. The polynomial \( sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1 \) changes sign in each of the intervals \( [-2,-1],[1 / 3,1 / 2],[3 / 2,2] \), so it has no zeros outside these intervals. Hence in the problem, the integrand is continuous on the three ranges of integration.

By the substitutions \( sunflower=1 /(1-cinnamon) \) and \( sunflower=1-1 / cinnamon \), the integrals over \( [1 / 101,1 / 11] \) and \( [101 / 100,11 / 10] \) are respectively converted into integrals over \( [-100,-10] \). The integrand
\[
chocolate(sunflower)=\left(\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right)^{2}
\]
is invariant under each of the substitutions \( sunflower \rightarrow 1 /(1-sunflower) \) and \( sunflower \rightarrow 1-1 / sunflower \). Hence the sum of the three given integrals is expressible as
\[
\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{sunflower^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-sunflower)^{2}}\right) d sunflower .
\]

But
\[
\frac{1}{chocolate(sunflower)}=\left(sunflower+1-\frac{1}{sunflower}-\frac{1}{sunflower-1}\right)^{2},
\]
so the last integral is of the form \( \int butterfly^{-2} d butterfly \). Hence its value is
\[
-\left.\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10},
\]
which is rational.

Solution 2. Set
\[
\begin{aligned}
marigold(cinnamon)= & \int_{-100}^{cinnamon}\left(\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right)^{2} d sunflower+\int_{\frac{1}{101}}^{1 /(1-cinnamon)}\left(\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right)^{2} d sunflower \\
& +\int_{\frac{101}{100}}^{1-1 / cinnamon}\left(\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right)^{2} d sunflower
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( -100 \leq cinnamon \leq-10 \). We want \( marigold(-10) \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
\[
marigold^{\prime}(cinnamon)=chocolate(cinnamon)+chocolate\left(\frac{1}{1-cinnamon}\right) \frac{1}{(1-cinnamon)^{2}}+chocolate\left(1-\frac{1}{cinnamon}\right) \frac{1}{cinnamon^{2}} .
\]

We find that \( chocolate(1 /(1-sunflower))=chocolate(1-1 / sunflower)=chocolate(sunflower)( \) in fact \( sunflower \mapsto 1 /(1-sunflower) \) and \( sunflower \mapsto 1-1 / sunflower \) are inverses of each other), so
\[
marigold(-10)=\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{sunflower^{2}-sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{sunflower^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-sunflower)^{2}}\right) d sunflower .
\]

The integrand equals
\[
\frac{sunflower^{4}-2 sunflower^{3}+3 sunflower^{2}-2 sunflower+1}{\left(sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1\right)^{2}} .
\]

We guess that this is the derivative of a quotient
\[
\frac{elephant sunflower^{2}+pineapple sunflower+telescope}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}
\]
and solve for the undetermined coefficients, obtaining \( elephant=-1, pineapple=1, telescope=0 \). Thus
\[
marigold(-10)=\left.\frac{-sunflower^{2}+sunflower}{sunflower^{3}-3 sunflower+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10}
\]
which is rational.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
\begin{gather*}
\int_{-100}^{-10} \left( \frac{knownconst^2 - knownconst}{knownconst^3 - 3knownconst + 1} \right)^2\,dknownconst + \\
\int_{\frac{1}{101}}^{\frac{1}{11}} \left( \frac{knownconst^2 - knownconst}{knownconst^3 - 3knownconst + 1} \right)^2\,dknownconst + \\
\int_{\frac{101}{100}}^{\frac{11}{10}} \left( \frac{knownconst^2 - knownconst}{knownconst^3 - 3knownconst + 1} \right)^2\,dknownconst
\end{gather*}
is a rational number.
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution 1. The polynomial \( knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1 \) changes sign in each of the intervals \( [-2,-1],[1 / 3,1 / 2],[3 / 2,2] \), so it has no zeros outside these intervals. Hence in the problem, the integrand is continuous on the three ranges of integration.

By the substitutions \( knownconst=1 /(1-timeless) \) and \( knownconst=1-1 / timeless \), the integrals over \( [1 / 101,1 / 11] \) and \( [101 / 100,11 / 10] \) are respectively converted into integrals over \( [-100,-10] \). The integrand
\[
productfun(knownconst)=\left(\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right)^{2}
\]
is invariant under each of the substitutions \( knownconst \rightarrow 1 /(1-knownconst) \) and \( knownconst \rightarrow 1-1 / knownconst \). Hence the sum of the three given integrals is expressible as
\[
\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{knownconst^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-knownconst)^{2}}\right) d knownconst .
\]

But
\[
\frac{1}{productfun(knownconst)}=\left(knownconst+1-\frac{1}{knownconst}-\frac{1}{knownconst-1}\right)^{2},
\]
so the last integral is of the form \( \int fixedvalue^{-2} d fixedvalue \). Hence its value is
\[
-\left.\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10},
\]
which is rational.
Solution 2. Set
\[
\begin{aligned}
steadyval(timeless)= & \int_{-100}^{timeless}\left(\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right)^{2} d knownconst+\int_{\frac{1}{101}}^{1 /(1-timeless)}\left(\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right)^{2} d knownconst \\
& +\int_{\frac{101}{100}}^{1-1 / timeless}\left(\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right)^{2} d knownconst
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( -100 \leq timeless \leq-10 \). We want \( steadyval(-10) \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
\[
steadyval^{\prime}(timeless)=productfun(timeless)+productfun\left(\frac{1}{1-timeless}\right) \frac{1}{(1-timeless)^{2}}+productfun\left(1-\frac{1}{timeless}\right) \frac{1}{timeless^{2}} .
\]

We find that \( productfun(1 /(1-knownconst))=productfun(1-1 / knownconst)=productfun(knownconst)( \) in fact \( knownconst \mapsto 1 /(1-knownconst) \) and \( knownconst \mapsto 1-1 / knownconst \) are inverses of each other), so
\[
steadyval(-10)=\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{knownconst^{2}-knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{knownconst^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-knownconst)^{2}}\right) d knownconst .
\]

The integrand equals
\[
\frac{knownconst^{4}-2 knownconst^{3}+3 knownconst^{2}-2 knownconst+1}{\left(knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1\right)^{2}} .
\]

We guess that this is the derivative of a quotient
\[
\frac{exponentc knownconst^{2}+intercept knownconst+variating}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}
\]
and solve for the undetermined coefficients, obtaining \( exponentc=-1, intercept=1, variating=0 \). Thus
\[
steadyval(-10)=\left.\frac{-knownconst^{2}+knownconst}{knownconst^{3}-3 knownconst+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10}
\]
which is rational.
>>>

garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. The polynomial \( qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1 \) changes sign in each of the intervals \( [-2,-1],[1 / 3,1 / 2],[3 / 2,2] \), so it has no zeros outside these intervals. Hence in the problem, the integrand is continuous on the three ranges of integration.

By the substitutions \( qzxwvtnp=1 /(1-hjgrksla) \) and \( qzxwvtnp=1-1 / hjgrksla \), the integrals over \( [1 / 101,1 / 11] \) and \( [101 / 100,11 / 10] \) are respectively converted into integrals over \( [-100,-10] \). The integrand
\[
lfkndpwo(qzxwvtnp)=\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right)^{2}
\]
is invariant under each of the substitutions \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow 1 /(1-qzxwvtnp) \) and \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow 1-1 / qzxwvtnp \). Hence the sum of the three given integrals is expressible as
\[
\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-qzxwvtnp)^{2}}\right) d qzxwvtnp .
\]

But
\[
\frac{1}{lfkndpwo(qzxwvtnp)}=\left(qzxwvtnp+1-\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}-\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp-1}\right)^{2},
\]
so the last integral is of the form \( \int mvdljrqe^{-2} d mvdljrqe \). Hence its value is
\[
-\left.\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10},
\]
which is rational.

Solution 2. Set
\[
\begin{aligned}
sbhrmkju(hjgrksla)= & \int_{-100}^{hjgrksla}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right)^{2} d qzxwvtnp+\int_{\frac{1}{101}}^{1 /(1-hjgrksla)}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right)^{2} d qzxwvtnp \\
& +\int_{\frac{101}{100}}^{1-1 / hjgrksla}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right)^{2} d qzxwvtnp
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( -100 \leq hjgrksla \leq-10 \). We want \( sbhrmkju(-10) \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
\[
sbhrmkju^{\prime}(hjgrksla)=lfkndpwo(hjgrksla)+lfkndpwo\left(\frac{1}{1-hjgrksla}\right) \frac{1}{(1-hjgrksla)^{2}}+lfkndpwo\left(1-\frac{1}{hjgrksla}\right) \frac{1}{hjgrksla^{2}} .
\]

We find that \( lfkndpwo(1 /(1-qzxwvtnp))=lfkndpwo(1-1 / qzxwvtnp)=lfkndpwo(qzxwvtnp)( \) in fact \( qzxwvtnp \mapsto 1 /(1-qzxwvtnp) \) and \( qzxwvtnp \mapsto 1-1 / qzxwvtnp \) are inverses of each other), so
\[
sbhrmkju(-10)=\int_{-100}^{-10}\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right)^{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp^{2}}+\frac{1}{(1-qzxwvtnp)^{2}}\right) d qzxwvtnp .
\]

The integrand equals
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp^{4}-2 qzxwvtnp^{3}+3 qzxwvtnp^{2}-2 qzxwvtnp+1}{\left(qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1\right)^{2}} .
\]

We guess that this is the derivative of a quotient
\[
\frac{gdvplzre qzxwvtnp^{2}+nqxdrsaf qzxwvtnp+twlmgbvi}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}
\]
and solve for the undetermined coefficients, obtaining \( gdvplzre=-1, nqxdrsaf=1, twlmgbvi=0 \). Thus
\[
sbhrmkju(-10)=\left.\frac{-qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp+1}\right|_{-100} ^{-10}
\]
which is rational.

kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
I\;=\;\int_{-150}^{-15}\!\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3x+1}\right)^{2}dx 
\;\;\;+
\int_{\frac1{151}}^{\frac1{16}}\!\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3x+1}\right)^{2}dx 
\;\;\;+
\int_{\frac{151}{150}}^{\frac{16}{15}}\!\left(\frac{x^{2}-x}{x^{3}-3x+1}\right)^{2}dx .
\]
Show that $I$ is a rational number and determine its exact value.
kernel_variant.solution:
Define Q(x)=((x^2-x)/(x^3-3x+1))^2.

Step 1 (invariance).
The Mobius transformations
T_1(x)=1/(1-x),  T_2(x)=1-1/x
are mutual inverses and satisfy Q(T_1(x))=Q(T_2(x))=Q(x).  A direct substitution verifies this.

Step 2 (sending the positive intervals to the negative one).
Note that
T_1([-150,-15])=[1/(1+150),1/(1+15)]=[1/151,1/16],
T_2([-150,-15])=[1-1/(-150),1-1/(-15)]=[151/150,16/15].
Hence performing T_1 in the second integral and T_2 in the third
integral transports both to the interval [-150,-15] and multiplies the
integrands by the appropriate Jacobians:

I=\int _{-150}^{-15} Q(x)(1+1/x^2+1/(1-x)^2) dx.

Step 3 (recognising a derivative).
An explicit calculation gives

Q(x)(1+1/x^2+1/(1-x)^2)
 = d/dx[(-x^2+x)/(x^3-3x+1)].

Step 4 (Fundamental Theorem of Calculus).
Therefore

I=[(-x^2+x)/(x^3-3x+1)]_{x=-150}^{x=-15}
 = F(-15)-F(-150)
where F(x)=(-x^2+x)/(x^3-3x+1).

Step 5 (endpoint evaluation).
Compute the two fractions:

F(-15)=(-225-15)/(-3375+45+1)=(-240)/(-3329)=240/3329,
F(-150)=(-22500-150)/(-3375000+450+1)=(-22650)/(-3374549)=22650/3374549.
Hence

I=240/3329 - 22650/3374549
 = 734489910/11233873621.

In particular, I is rational, and

I=734489910/11233873621.

core_steps: Observe that Q(x)=((x^2−x)/(x^3−3x+1))^2 is invariant under the Möbius maps T₁:x↦1/(1−x) and T₂:x↦1−1/x.
Use T₁ and T₂ to send the two ‘positive’ intervals onto the chosen ‘negative’ interval, so the three given integrals collapse to one integral over that single interval.
After substitution the common integrand becomes Q(x)·(1+1/x²+1/(1−x)²).
Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to evaluate the integral via endpoint values, yielding a rational number.

slot1.description: Left endpoint magnitude of the negative-interval (must be > slot2 and lie left of −1 so the denominator has no zero there).

slot2.description: Right endpoint magnitude of the negative-interval (positive number < slot1 but still <1 so its negative lies left of −1).

slot3.description: The two positive intervals’ endpoints, determined uniquely from slots 1 and 2 by the maps T₁ and T₂: 1/(1+slot1), 1/(1+slot2), (1+slot1)/slot1, (1+slot2)/slot2.
